---
title: "Reader Path"
---

Use this path as the guided textbook order. It starts with the smallest concepts, then moves toward harder structure, higher dimensions, and applications.

The indexes are not the book. They are the reference shelves you open when a lesson names a theorem id, dictionary term, paper, or source file.

## Before You Start

- Read the [theorem status legend](status.qmd).
- Use [Verify A Claim](verify_claim.qmd) when you need to audit one theorem id, term, widget, glyph, or paper claim end to end.

::: {.warning-box}
Targets, widgets, Python examples, and diagrams are not proofs. A theorem is Lean-proved only when generated theorem data and the local Lean build say so.
:::

## Part I: Learn The S1 Core

These chapters are the first textbook spine. Read them in order before jumping to higher dimensions.

<div class="learning-ladder">

<div class="learning-step">
<strong>Lesson 1: finite circles.</strong> Learn that `C_n` is a finite cyclic address space. Use the widget to see how labels wrap.
</div>

<div class="learning-step">
<strong>Lesson 2: rotation.</strong> Learn that motion on `C_n` is addition modulo `n`, and that composing rotations adds strides.
</div>

<div class="learning-step">
<strong>Lesson 3: coils.</strong> Learn that repeated rotation traces an orbit, and that closure is the return to the starting address.
</div>

<div class="learning-step">
<strong>Lesson 4: period and primes.</strong> Learn why `gcd(n,k)` controls cycle count and why prime circles make every nonzero stride a full coil.
</div>

<div class="learning-step">
<strong>Lesson 5: winding.</strong> Learn why a residue alone forgets how many turns occurred, and how a lifted coordinate records quotient plus remainder.
</div>

</div>

| Step | Page | What To Check |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | [Finite circles](chapters/S1/01_finite_circles.qmd) | Can you reduce any integer to its node in `C_n`? |
| 2 | [Rotation as addition](chapters/S1/02_rotation_as_addition.qmd) | Can you predict `rot(n,a)(rot(n,b)(x))` before using the widget? |
| 3 | [Coils and closure](chapters/S1/03_coils_orbits_closure.qmd) | Can you list the orbit for a chosen start and stride? |
| 4 | [Period and primes](chapters/S1/04_period_gcd_prime_full_coils.qmd) | Can you compute the period from `gcd(n,k)`? |
| 5 | [Winding lift](chapters/S1/05_winding_lift.qmd) | Can you separate total steps into turns and residue? |

## Part II: Add Signs, Factors, And Higher Shapes

Once S1 is comfortable, use the dimensional ladder. Each page should explain what is proved, what is only modeled, and what remains a warning boundary.

| Layer | Page | What It Adds |
| --- | --- | --- |
| S0 | [Opposition](chapters/S0/opposition.qmd) | Two-point sign/opposition vocabulary. |
| S2 | [Suspended circles](chapters/S2/suspended_circles.qmd) | A first bridge from circular layers to finite sphere-like grids. |
| S3 | [Quaternions](chapters/S3/quaternions.qmd) | Rotation algebra and spin sign ambiguity in a bounded checked spine. |
| S3 | [Hopf coils](chapters/S3/hopf_coils.qmd) | Visible base coordinates with hidden phase, without overclaiming full topology. |
| S4-S6 | [Suspension Euler parity](chapters/S4_S6/suspension_euler_parity.qmd) | Common suspension-count and Euler bridge. |
| S7 | [Octonionic layer](chapters/S7/octonionic_layer.qmd) | Bounded octonion-model facts and nonassociativity warnings. |
| S15 | [Future Hopf horizon](chapters/S15/future_hopf_horizon.qmd) | Future-facing horizon facts and warning boundaries. |

## Part III: Read Applications As Case Studies

Application chapters are not detached essays. Read them as case studies that reuse the same S1 language: address, rotation, period, phase, closure, and proof status.

- [Data analysis](chapters/applications/data_analysis.qmd): periodic coordinates, deterministic fixtures, and benchmark guardrails.
- [Compute](chapters/applications/compute.qmd): cyclic addresses, stride layouts, schedules, and proof-carrying compute language.
- [AI](chapters/applications/ai.qmd): phase channels, memory slots, adapter blocks, and explicit benchmark requirements.
- [Rendering](chapters/applications/rendering.qmd): direction bins and scaffolded visual-compute tracks.

## Part IV: Use The Source Trail

- Papers live in [`papers/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/papers).
- Lean proofs live in [`Circle/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/Circle) and sidecars under [`sidecars/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/sidecars).
- Python reference models live in [`circle_math/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/circle_math).
- Manifests live in [`manifests/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/manifests).
- Dictionary source lives in [`dictionary/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/dictionary).
- Open the [theorem index](theorems.qmd) when a page names a theorem id.
- Open the [dictionary index](dictionary.qmd) when a page uses project vocabulary.
- Use the [target index](targets.qmd) for planned, exploratory, blocked, or edge-problem work.

## Part V: Verify Locally

The repository verification path is:

```bash
make check
make site-render
make living-book-check
```

These commands are the local source of truth for the public site, proof metadata, sidecars, dictionary links, target links, and fake-proof guardrails.

For a reader-facing checklist, use [Verify A Claim](verify_claim.qmd).

The public site deploy path is GitHub Pages. Deployment is generated from `site/_site/` only after the GitHub Actions workflow has built Lean, run source checks, and rendered Quarto.

<script type="module" src="widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
